**#36098** 1:05 PM 🔵 **Saga 14 MLX Backend Design Already Documented**

Investigation of the next saga revealed that Saga 14 (MLX backend) already has comprehensive design documentation in docs/using-mlx.md. The document outlines a three-phase implementation: first building mlpl-mlx as a sibling runtime target to mlpl-rt with MLX-backed primitives, then adding device placement syntax with device("mlx")/device("cpu") scoped forms, and finally enabling training on MLX with the existing Model DSL transparently dispatching to MLX tensors. The design emphasizes "one source, many backends" where the same .mlpl code runs on CPU, MLX, or CUDA with only device placement changes. MLX was selected as the first accelerator backend because the project baseline is Apple Silicon dev laptops and MLX's array semantics already align with MLPL's DenseArray (row-major, shape-labeled, broadcast rules). The current saga state shows mlpl-tiny-lm completed at step 9, ready for archiving, with 13 prior sagas already in the archive directory.

**#36100** " 🔵 **Agentrail Step Structure and Metadata Format**

Examination of Saga 13's step structure reveals the agentrail step organization pattern. Each of the 9 completed steps lives in a numbered directory (001-embedding-layer through 009-tiny-lm-release-v0100) containing three files: prompt.md with detailed implementation instructions, step.toml with metadata tracking, and summary.md capturing completion notes. The step.toml file tracks comprehensive metadata including number, slug identifier, completion status, role classification (production/deterministic/validation/meta), task type for categorization, creation and completion timestamps, and an array of associated git commit SHAs. The prompt.md format follows a structured TDD approach: numbered implementation requirements, tape-lowering specifications for gradient flow, explicit test cases (unit tests and gradchecks), quality gate references, and commit message guidance. This step format provides the template for creating Saga 14's steps once the current saga is archived.

**#36101** 1:06 PM ✅ **Saga 13 Tiny LM Archived to Timestamped Directory**

Saga 13 (mlpl-tiny-lm) has been successfully archived using the agentrail archive command with a descriptive reason documenting the v0.10.0 release. The complete saga state—including the 9-step plan, saga configuration, all step directories with their prompts and summaries, session logs, and trajectory data—was moved from the active .agentrail/ directory to a timestamped archive directory at .agentrail-archive/mlpl-tiny-lm-20260416T130558/. The archive includes an archive-reason.txt file capturing why the saga was archived. The .agentrail/ directory is now completely empty, providing a clean slate for initializing Saga 14 (MLX backend). This archival process preserves the complete history of how the Tiny LM milestone was achieved while maintaining the active workspace's focus on current work.

Read: ~374, Work: 🛠️ 1,343

**docs/milestone-mlx-backend.md**
| ID | Time | T | Title | Read | Work |
|----|------|---|-------|------|------|

**#36113** 1:14 PM 🟣 **Saga 14 MLX Backend Implementation Plan Created and Initialized**

Saga 14 implementation plan was created and initialized to bring MLX acceleration to MLPL on Apple Silicon. The 10-step plan spans five phases: first building the mlpl-mlx runtime crate that ports core primitives (matmul, elementwise ops, reductions, softmax) to MLX-backed arrays while preserving LabeledShape semantics; second, adding device("mlx") { } scoped syntax through a new Expr::Device AST variant with evaluator dispatch; third, implementing full autograd support with tape-lowered backward passes and gradcheck parity; fourth, creating an MLX variant of the Saga 13 Tiny LM demo targeting >=5x speedup; and fifth, shipping tutorial documentation and v0.11.0 release. The design ensures source compatibility across backends—CPU, MLX (Saga 14), and future CUDA (Saga 17)—with device placement as the only change. Quality requirements include feature-gated MLX tests (macOS aarch64 only) to keep Linux CI green. The highest implementation risk is Step 006 where MLX's lazy graph and MLPL's autograd tape must coexist, requiring either wholesale adoption of mlx-rs vjp/grad or hand-written backward passes per primitive. Target speedup is 5-50x over interpreter on M-class laptops.

Read: ~620, Work: 🛠️ 7,764

| ID | Time | T | Title | Read | Work |

**#36115** 1:37 PM ✅ **All 10 Saga 14 Steps Created and Added to Agentrail**

All 10 implementation steps for Saga 14 MLX Backend were created as detailed prompt files and added to the agentrail saga in sequence. The steps follow a structured TDD progression across five phases: Phase 1 builds the mlpl-mlx runtime crate as a sibling to mlpl-rt, porting matmul first (step 001) then elementwise/shape ops (step 002) and finally reductions/softmax (step 003), each with parity tests against the CPU path within documented fp32 tolerance. Phase 2 exposes device placement through new device("mlx") { body } scoped syntax with an Expr::Device AST variant (step 004) and wires Model DSL apply/params through the active backend with to_device movement helpers (step 005). Phase 3 is the highest-risk phase implementing tape-lowered autograd on MLX (step 006) with a TDD-time decision between leveraging mlx-rs vjp/grad or hand-writing per-primitive backwards, then integrating optimizers and train loops (step 007) so OptimizerState holds MLX tensors. Phase 4 proves the end-to-end speedup with demos/tiny_lm_mlx.mlpl (step 008) targeting >=5x warm-path wall-clock improvement and extending mlpl-bench with MLX rows. Phase 5 ships user-facing artifacts: a "Running on MLX" tutorial lesson and conversion of docs/using-mlx.md from design sketch to reference (step 009), then the v0.11.0 release tag and pages deployment verification (step 010). Each step prompt follows the established pattern with numbered requirements, TDD guidance, quality gates, and commit message references. The saga is now active at step 1 with the complete roadmap loaded.

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, use Config tool.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- less-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (triggers) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/triggers.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- mw-cp: Mike Wright's Checkpoint Process — pre-commit checks, doc updates, detailed commit, and push
- claude-mem:make-plan: Create an implementation plan with documentation discovery
- claude-mem:do: Execute a plan using subagents for implementation
- claude-mem:mem-search: Search claude-mem's persistent cross-session memory database. Use when user asks "did we already solve this?", "how did we do X last time?", or needs work from previous sessions.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
=== PLAN ===
# MLX Backend Milestone (Saga 14, v0.11.0)

## Why this exists

Saga 13 proved the platform thesis on CPU: a few lines of MLPL
train a tiny transformer LM, generate text, and render its own
attention. The next bottleneck is speed. The Saga 13 Tiny LM
trains at interpreter pace on an M-class laptop; an accelerator
backend is the proper lever.

Apple Silicon is this project's baseline dev hardware, and MLX
is its native array framework: lazy graph execution, automatic
operator fusion, unified CPU+GPU memory, and no kernel-per-device
dance. Its array semantics already agree with MLPL's
`DenseArray` (row-major, shape-labeled, consistent broadcast
rules). Saga 14 introduces MLX as MLPL's first accelerator
backend behind the same source surface: a program that trains on
CPU should train on MLX without source changes.

CUDA (Saga 17) is the second accelerator backend. The MLPL
source surface shipped in Saga 14 is the same surface CUDA will
land behind. See `docs/using-mlx.md` for the user-facing design
sketch; this doc is the implementation plan.

## Non-goals

- **No CUDA.** Saga 17.
- **No distributed execution.** Saga 17.
- **No LoRA / quantization.** Saga 15.
- **No new Model DSL layers.** The existing `linear`, `chain`,
  `residual`, `rms_norm`, `attention`, `causal_attention`,
  `embed`, `sinusoidal_encoding`, activations, and
  `cross_entropy` ship on MLX as-is.
- **No checkpoint format.** Still deferred to Saga 15+.
- **No pretraining claims.** Target is the Saga 13 Tiny LM demo
  running faster, not a bigger model.

## Quality requirements (every step)

Identical to Saga 13:

1. TDD: failing test first, then implementation, then refactor.
2. Quality gates must all pass before commit:
   - `cargo test`
   - `cargo clippy --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings`
   - `cargo fmt --all` + `cargo fmt --all -- --check`
   - `markdown-checker -f "**/*.md"` if docs touched
   - `sw-checklist`
3. Use `/mw-cp` checkpoint process.
4. Push immediately after commit.
5. Web UI changes rebuild `pages/` via `scripts/build-pages.sh`.
6. `.agentrail/` changes are committed whenever they change.
7. MLX-feature tests are gated so CI on non-Apple-Silicon hosts
   still passes (`#[cfg(all(target_os = "macos",
   target_arch = "aarch64", feature = "mlx"))]` or the closest
   equivalent supported by `mlx-rs`). Where MLX is unavailable,
   the CPU path remains authoritative.

## What already exists

- `mlpl-rt` runtime target (scalar, array, fncall, variable,
  labels, matmul with static contraction check) used by both
  `mlpl!` proc macro and `mlpl-build` CLI.
- `mlpl-parity-tests` harness proving bit-for-bit agreement
  between interpreter and compile-to-Rust paths on 9 programs.
- `mlpl-bench` Criterion harness comparing interpreter vs
  compiled MLPL (9.05x measured speedup on 100x100 reshape+
  reduce).
- `mlpl-lower-rs` path-configurable AST -> `TokenStream` codegen
  (the `runtime_prefix` knob makes swapping runtimes cheap).
- Full Saga 11/13 Model DSL, autograd, optimizers, and the
  Tiny LM end-to-end demo (`demos/tiny_lm.mlpl`).
- Labeled axes and structured `EvalError::ShapeMismatch`
  propagate through every existing op.

## Phase 1 -- mlpl-mlx runtime target (3 steps)

### Step 001 -- mlpl-mlx crate skeleton + first primitive
New `crates/mlpl-mlx` sibling to `mlpl-rt`. `mlx-rs` dependency
behind a `mlx` Cargo feature. Ports exactly one primitive --
`matmul(a, b)` -- backed by MLX's `matmul` and returning an
MLX array wrapped in the same `DenseArray` / `LabeledShape`
shape as `mlpl-rt`. Parity test vs `mlpl-rt::matmul` on a
`[8, 4] @ [4, 8]` fixture (bit-for-bit or within a documented
fp32 tolerance; decide during TDD). `cargo test -p mlpl-mlx
--features mlx` gates the test; default build stays green on
non-Apple hosts.

### Step 002 -- core elementwise + shape ops on MLX
Port the shape- and element-level primitives the existing
interpreter uses most: `add`/`sub`/`mul`/`div`/`neg`, `exp`/
`log`/`tanh`/`sigmoid`/`relu`, `reshape`, `transpose`. Each
primitive lands with a unit test + parity test vs `mlpl-rt`.
Labels propagate identically -- the MLX runtime does not own
`LabeledShape`, it borrows `mlpl-core`'s. Gradcheck is not in
scope yet (step 006).

### Step 003 -- reductions + softmax + log-softmax on MLX
`reduce_add`/`reduce_mul`/`mean`/`argmax` (axis-aware, label-
aware), `softmax`, `log_softmax`, and `cross_entropy`.
Numerical-stability invariants from the CPU path (max-
subtraction log-softmax) carry over. Parity tests on `[4, 3]`
and `[2, 3, 5]` fixtures. At the end of this phase the MLX
runtime can compute forward passes of every primitive the Tiny
LM uses.

## Phase 2 -- Device placement + backend dispatch (2 steps)

### Step 004 -- device("...") scoped form + parser surface
New `device("mlx") { body }` scoped form in the parser/AST
(`Expr::Device`), mirroring `experiment`'s shape. Inside the
block, evaluator dispatches array allocations and ops through
the MLX runtime when the `mlx` feature is enabled; falls back
to CPU (with a one-time warning) otherwise. Labels and shapes
propagate identically across the boundary. Tests: parser
round-trip, evaluator returns the same shape+labels as the CPU
path, `device("cpu") { }` is a no-op and works on every host.

### Step 005 -- backend dispatch in the Model DSL
`apply(model, X)` and `params(model)` route through the active
backend when `X` was allocated inside a `device("mlx")` block.
A model owns its parameters on one device -- moving between
devices requires an explicit `to_device("cpu")` / `to_device
("mlx")` builtin (single-direction helpers that copy tensors;
tests cover round-trip equality). The Saga 11 models
(`linear`, `chain`, `residual`, `rms_norm`, `attention`,
`causal_attention`) produce bit-for-bit (or tolerance-bounded)
equivalent outputs on both devices for fixed seeds.

## Phase 3 -- Autograd on MLX (2 steps)

### Step 006 -- tape-lowered ops on MLX + gradcheck parity
`grad(expr, wrt)` works inside a `device("mlx")` block. The
autograd tape's primitives (`add`, `mul`, `matmul`, `exp`,
`log`, `relu`, `tanh`, `sigmoid`, `softmax`, `sum`, `mean`,
`transpose`, `reshape`) all ship MLX-backed backward paths,
each gradcheck-verified against finite differences on fixtures
matching Saga 9's. Where MLX's own `vjp` / `grad` helps,
`mlpl-mlx` is free to use it; where it doesn't, we hand-write
the backward. Either way, the invariant is that
`grad(expr, wrt)` on MLX matches the CPU path to within a
documented tolerance.

### Step 007 -- optimizers + train { } on MLX
`adam`, `momentum_sgd`, `train N { body }`, `last_losses`,
`experiment "name" { }`, and the `_metric` capture all work
unchanged inside `device("mlx") { }`. The `OptimizerState` map
holds MLX-typed tensors when the surrounding scope is MLX.
Parity test: one Adam step on the Saga 11 `tiny_mlp` demo
produces the same parameter update (within tolerance) on CPU
and MLX.

## Phase 4 -- Tiny LM on MLX + benchmarks (1 step)

### Step 008 -- tiny LM MLX variant + bench harness
`demos/tiny_lm_mlx.mlpl` wraps the Saga 13 `demos/tiny_lm.mlpl`
body in `device("mlx") { ... }` and otherwise changes nothing.
Measurable outcome: identical-shape loss curve, >=5x wall-clock
speedup over the interpreter CPU path on an M-class laptop
(target 10-50x per `docs/using-mlx.md`, but 5x is the go / no-go
gate). Extend `mlpl-bench` with an MLX row on the existing
reshape+reduce workload and the Tiny LM training-step workload.
Document the numbers in `docs/benchmarks.md`.

## Phase 5 -- Docs + release (2 steps)

### Step 009 -- tutorial lesson + using-mlx.md retrospective
New web REPL tutorial lesson **"Running on MLX"** that walks
from a `device("cpu") { randn(7, [1024, 64]) }` baseline to the
same expression wrapped in `device("mlx") { ... }`, then shows
a tiny training loop swap. Update `docs/using-mlx.md` from
"design sketch" to "reference" and strip the `> Status: planned`
disclaimer; add a retrospective section summarizing what
actually shipped vs the sketch. Update `docs/status.md`
one-liner and `docs/saga.md` Saga 14 entry. Rebuild `pages/`
via `scripts/build-pages.sh` and commit both source and built
artifact in the same commit.

### Step 010 -- release v0.11.0
Bump workspace version to `0.11.0`. Release commit summarizes
`mlpl-mlx` runtime, `device("...") { }` scoped form,
`to_device(...)` movement helpers, autograd + optimizers on
MLX, and the Tiny LM MLX variant with its measured speedup.
Tag `v0.11.0`. Push commit and tag. Verify the pages workflow
deploys the updated tutorial list.

## Dependencies and risk

- Step 001 depends on `mlx-rs` building cleanly on the dev
  laptop; pin a known-good version in `Cargo.toml`. Risk: MLX
  crate ABI changes between versions. Mitigation: pin minor.
- Step 004 introduces `Expr::Device`. Risk: the scoped form
  interacts with `experiment { }` and `train { }` nesting.
  Mitigation: explicit tests for `experiment { device { train
  { } } }` and the swapped nesting.
- Step 006 is the highest-risk step. MLX's lazy graph and
  MLPL's tape are two autograd systems; we only get to pick
  one per expression. Decide during TDD whether to lean on
  `mlx-rs`'s `vjp` / `grad` wholesale or hand-write backward
  per primitive. Either path must produce the same gradients
  (within tolerance) as the CPU tape.
- Step 008 risk: MLX compile overhead on the first call may
  dominate the Tiny LM's wall clock at this size. Mitigation:
  bench harness reports both cold and warm timings; go/no-go
  is warm-path speedup.
- Cross-platform CI risk: every MLX test must be feature- and
  target-gated so `cargo test` on Linux CI stays green. This
  is an invariant across every step, not a single-step task.

=== PLAN ===
# MLX Backend Milestone (Saga 14, v0.11.0)

## Why this exists

Saga 13 proved the platform thesis on CPU: a few lines of MLPL
train a tiny transformer LM, generate text, and render its own
attention. The next bottleneck is speed. The Saga 13 Tiny LM
trains at interpreter pace on an M-class laptop; an accelerator
backend is the proper lever.

Apple Silicon is this project's baseline dev hardware, and MLX
is its native array framework: lazy graph execution, automatic
operator fusion, unified CPU+GPU memory, and no kernel-per-device
dance. Its array semantics already agree with MLPL's
`DenseArray` (row-major, shape-labeled, consistent broadcast
rules). Saga 14 introduces MLX as MLPL's first accelerator
backend behind the same source surface: a program that trains on
CPU should train on MLX without source changes.

CUDA (Saga 17) is the second accelerator backend. The MLPL
source surface shipped in Saga 14 is the same surface CUDA will
land behind. See `docs/using-mlx.md` for the user-facing design
sketch; this doc is the implementation plan.

## Non-goals

- **No CUDA.** Saga 17.
- **No distributed execution.** Saga 17.
- **No LoRA / quantization.** Saga 15.
- **No new Model DSL layers.** The existing `linear`, `chain`,
  `residual`, `rms_norm`, `attention`, `causal_attention`,
  `embed`, `sinusoidal_encoding`, activations, and
  `cross_entropy` ship on MLX as-is.
- **No checkpoint format.** Still deferred to Saga 15+.
- **No pretraining claims.** Target is the Saga 13 Tiny LM demo
  running faster, not a bigger model.

## Quality requirements (every step)

Identical to Saga 13:

1. TDD: failing test first, then implementation, then refactor.
2. Quality gates must all pass before commit:
   - `cargo test`
   - `cargo clippy --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings`
   - `cargo fmt --all` + `cargo fmt --all -- --check`
   - `markdown-checker -f "**/*.md"` if docs touched
   - `sw-checklist`
3. Use `/mw-cp` checkpoint process.
4. Push immediately after commit.
5. Web UI changes rebuild `pages/` via `scripts/build-pages.sh`.
6. `.agentrail/` changes are committed whenever they change.
7. MLX-feature tests are gated so CI on non-Apple-Silicon hosts
   still passes (`#[cfg(all(target_os = "macos",
   target_arch = "aarch64", feature = "mlx"))]` or the closest
   equivalent supported by `mlx-rs`). Where MLX is unavailable,
   the CPU path remains authoritative.

## What already exists

- `mlpl-rt` runtime target (scalar, array, fncall, variable,
  labels, matmul with static contraction check) used by both
  `mlpl!` proc macro and `mlpl-build` CLI.
- `mlpl-parity-tests` harness proving bit-for-bit agreement
  between interpreter and compile-to-Rust paths on 9 programs.
- `mlpl-bench` Criterion harness comparing interpreter vs
  compiled MLPL (9.05x measured speedup on 100x100 reshape+
  reduce).
- `mlpl-lower-rs` path-configurable AST -> `TokenStream` codegen
  (the `runtime_prefix` knob makes swapping runtimes cheap).
- Full Saga 11/13 Model DSL, autograd, optimizers, and the
  Tiny LM end-to-end demo (`demos/tiny_lm.mlpl`).
- Labeled axes and structured `EvalError::ShapeMismatch`
  propagate through every existing op.

1	# Using MLPL with MLX (Apple Silicon)
2	
3	> **Status:** planned -- Saga 14 (`v0.11` target). Not yet
4	> shipped. This doc sketches the intended API so users and
5	> contributors can plan around it; actual code lands with the
6	> saga. Treat everything below as design, not reference.
7	
8	## Why MLX
9	
10	MLX is Apple's array framework for Apple Silicon: lazy graph
11	execution, automatic operator fusion, unified CPU+GPU memory, and
12	no kernel-per-device dance. It's the right first accelerator
13	backend for MLPL because:
14	
15	- Apple Silicon dev laptops are the baseline for this project.
16	- MLX's array type already matches MLPL's `DenseArray` semantics
17	  (row-major, shape-labeled, broadcast rules agree).
18	- Lazy execution composes cleanly with MLPL's existing
19	  tape-based autograd -- the autograd tape builds the graph the
20	  fuser already wants.
21	
22	CUDA (Saga 17) is the second accelerator backend. See
23	`docs/using-cuda.md`. The plan is for both backends to share the
24	same MLPL surface -- a program that trains on MLX should train on
25	CUDA without source changes.
26	
27	## Saga 14 shape
28	
29	`docs/plan.md` breaks Saga 14 into roughly three phases:
30	
31	1. **`mlpl-mlx` runtime target.** Sibling to `mlpl-rt`. Re-exports
32	   the MLX-backed equivalents of every `mlpl-rt` primitive --
33	   `mlx_rt::matmul`, `mlx_rt::reduce_add`, etc. A feature flag on
34	   the `mlpl` facade crate selects the backend.
35	2. **Device placement + movement.** A `device("mlx")` /
36	   `device("cpu")` scoped form in the language, mirroring PyTorch's
37	   `.to(device)` but reifying the choice in the AST so the
38	   compiler can fuse across it. Labels and shapes propagate across
39	   the device boundary unchanged.
40	3. **Training on MLX.** The Model DSL's `apply`, `grad`, and
41	   optimizer surface pick up MLX tensors under the `mlx`
42	   feature. The Saga 13 Tiny LM demo gets an MLX variant that
43	   trains roughly 10-50x faster on an M-class laptop than the
44	   CPU interpreter or compiled-to-rust path.
45	
46	## Intended API (not shipped)
47	
48	```mlpl
49	# Place a fresh tensor on the MLX device
50	X : [batch, feat] = device("mlx") { randn(7, [1024, 64]) }
51	
52	# Existing Model DSL, transparently dispatched to MLX
53	mdl = chain(linear(64, 256, 0), relu_layer(), linear(256, 64, 1))
54	Y   = apply(mdl, X)
55	
56	# Training loop, identical surface to the CPU version
57	train 100 {
58	  adam(cross_entropy(apply(mdl, X), targets), mdl,
59	       0.01, 0.9, 0.999, 0.00000001);
60	  loss_metric = cross_entropy(apply(mdl, X), targets)
61	}
62	```
63	
64	The intent is that swapping `device("mlx")` for `device("cpu")`
65	(or dropping it) is the only change needed between MLX-accelerated
66	and CPU-interpreted runs. Shape checking, labels, and the
67	`:describe` / `:vars` output stay identical.
68	
69	## Fine-tuning use case
70	
71	> **Status:** even more speculative than the above. Landing this
72	> depends on Saga 15 (LoRA / QLoRA / quantization).
73	
74	Fine-tuning a small pretrained LM with LoRA on Apple Silicon is
75	the planned flagship MLX demo. Rough shape of the workflow:
76	
77	1. Load a pretrained checkpoint into MLPL's Model DSL (requires a
78	   checkpoint format -- Saga 13 deliberately deferred this).
79	2. Freeze the base-model parameters via a flag on `param[...]`
80	   (deferred to Saga 15).
81	3. Attach low-rank adapters on the attention layers (`lora(...)`
82	   as a new layer kind in the Model DSL, also Saga 15).
83	4. Train the adapters with `adam` on MLX.
84	
85	Until Sagas 14 + 15 land, the practical fine-tuning path is the
86	CPU Saga 13 Tiny LM, which trains in seconds on a tiny corpus and
87	is a good place to learn the API shape.
88	
89	## What you can do today
90	
91	- **Prototype the shape of a model** with the current Model DSL.
92	  Use `:describe mdl` and `:vars` to confirm shapes and label
93	  propagation -- exactly what will transfer to MLX once that
94	  backend lands.
95	- **Measure the CPU baseline.** `cargo bench -p mlpl-bench` runs
96	  the interpreter vs the compile-to-Rust path. The MLX numbers
97	  will land in the same harness when Saga 14 ships, so you'll
98	  have a direct comparison.
99	- **Read `docs/saga.md`** for the current saga status. If Saga 14
100	  has flipped to `IN PROGRESS` the API in this doc may already be
101	  stale -- the saga's per-step prompts are the authoritative
102	  source until the retrospective milestone doc replaces them.
103	
104	## Why *not* just use MLX directly from Rust?
105	
106	You can. `mlx-rs` exists. MLPL's value proposition is:
107	
108	- **One source, many backends.** The same `.mlpl` source that
109	  trains on CPU trains on MLX trains on CUDA (Saga 17) trains in
110	  WASM (compile-to-rust, today). No duplicate codebases.
111	- **Labeled shapes and structured errors.** Writing MLX directly
112	  means carrying positional shapes in your head again.
113	- **The REPL surface.** `:describe model` and the tutorial lesson
114	  flow don't exist on top of raw MLX.
115	
116	When Saga 14 ships, `docs/compiler-guide.md` will grow a section
117	on the `mlpl-mlx` feature flag. Until then: CPU-first, and watch
118	`docs/plan.md`.
119	
120	## Related
121	
122	- `docs/plan.md` -- Saga 14 plan (MLX backend)
123	- `docs/saga.md` -- live saga status
124	- `docs/using-cuda.md` -- the other accelerator backend (Saga 17)
125	- `docs/benchmarks.md` -- where MLX numbers will land once the
126	  backend ships

file.content:
# Using MLPL with MLX (Apple Silicon)

> **Status:** planned -- Saga 14 (`v0.11` target). Not yet
> shipped. This doc sketches the intended API so users and
> contributors can plan around it; actual code lands with the
> saga. Treat everything below as design, not reference.

## Why MLX

MLX is Apple's array framework for Apple Silicon: lazy graph
execution, automatic operator fusion, unified CPU+GPU memory, and
no kernel-per-device dance. It's the right first accelerator
backend for MLPL because:

- Apple Silicon dev laptops are the baseline for this project.
- MLX's array type already matches MLPL's `DenseArray` semantics
  (row-major, shape-labeled, broadcast rules agree).
- Lazy execution composes cleanly with MLPL's existing
  tape-based autograd -- the autograd tape builds the graph the
  fuser already wants.

CUDA (Saga 17) is the second accelerator backend. See
`docs/using-cuda.md`. The plan is for both backends to share the
same MLPL surface -- a program that trains on MLX should train on
CUDA without source changes.

## Saga 14 shape

`docs/plan.md` breaks Saga 14 into roughly three phases:

1. **`mlpl-mlx` runtime target.** Sibling to `mlpl-rt`. Re-exports
   the MLX-backed equivalents of every `mlpl-rt` primitive --
   `mlx_rt::matmul`, `mlx_rt::reduce_add`, etc. A feature flag on
   the `mlpl` facade crate selects the backend.
2. **Device placement + movement.** A `device("mlx")` /
   `device("cpu")` scoped form in the language, mirroring PyTorch's
   `.to(device)` but reifying the choice in the AST so the
   compiler can fuse across it. Labels and shapes propagate across
   the device boundary unchanged.
3. **Training on MLX.** The Model DSL's `apply`, `grad`, and
   optimizer surface pick up MLX tensors under the `mlx`
   feature. The Saga 13 Tiny LM demo gets an MLX variant that
   trains roughly 10-50x faster on an M-class laptop than the
   CPU interpreter or compiled-to-rust path.

## Intended API (not shipped)

```mlpl
# Place a fresh tensor on the MLX device
X : [batch, feat] = device("mlx") { randn(7, [1024, 64]) }

# Existing Model DSL, transparently dispatched to MLX
mdl = chain(linear(64, 256, 0), relu_layer(), linear(256, 64, 1))
Y   = apply(mdl, X)

# Training loop, identical surface to the CPU version
train 100 {
  adam(cross_entropy(apply(mdl, X), targets), mdl,
       0.01, 0.9, 0.999, 0.00000001);
  loss_metric = cross_entropy(apply(mdl, X), targets)
}
```

The intent is that swapping `device("mlx")` for `device("cpu")`
(or dropping it) is the only change needed between MLX-accelerated
and CPU-interpreted runs. Shape checking, labels, and the
`:describe` / `:vars` output stay identical.

## Fine-tuning use case

> **Status:** even more speculative than the above. Landing this
> depends on Saga 15 (LoRA / QLoRA / quantization).

Fine-tuning a small pretrained LM with LoRA on Apple Silicon is
the planned flagship MLX demo. Rough shape of the workflow:

1. Load a pretrained checkpoint into MLPL's Model DSL (requires a
   checkpoint format -- Saga 13 deliberately deferred this).
2. Freeze the base-model parameters via a flag on `param[...]`
   (deferred to Saga 15).
3. Attach low-rank adapters on the attention layers (`lora(...)`
   as a new layer kind in the Model DSL, also Saga 15).
4. Train the adapters with `adam` on MLX.

Until Sagas 14 + 15 land, the practical fine-tuning path is the
CPU Saga 13 Tiny LM, which trains in seconds on a tiny corpus and
is a good place to learn the API shape.

## What you can do today

- **Prototype the shape of a model** with the current Model DSL.
  Use `:describe mdl` and `:vars` to confirm shapes and label
  propagation -- exactly what will transfer to MLX once that
  backend lands.
- **Measure the CPU baseline.** `cargo bench -p mlpl-bench` runs
  the interpreter vs the compile-to-Rust path. The MLX numbers
  will land in the same harness when Saga 14 ships, so you'll
  have a direct comparison.
- **Read `docs/saga.md`** for the current saga status. If Saga 14
  has flipped to `IN PROGRESS` the API in this doc may already be
  stale -- the saga's per-step prompts are the authoritative
  source until the retrospective milestone doc replaces them.

## Why *not* just use MLX directly from Rust?

You can. `mlx-rs` exists. MLPL's value proposition is:

- **One source, many backends.** The same `.mlpl` source that
  trains on CPU trains on MLX trains on CUDA (Saga 17) trains in
  WASM (compile-to-rust, today). No duplicate codebases.
- **Labeled shapes and structured errors.** Writing MLX directly
  means carrying positional shapes in your head again.
- **The REPL surface.** `:describe model` and the tutorial lesson
  flow don't exist on top of raw MLX.

When Saga 14 ships, `docs/compiler-guide.md` will grow a section
on the `mlpl-mlx` feature flag. Until then: CPU-first, and watch
`docs/plan.md`.

## Related

- `docs/plan.md` -- Saga 14 plan (MLX backend)
- `docs/saga.md` -- live saga status
- `docs/using-cuda.md` -- the other accelerator backend (Saga 17)
- `docs/benchmarks.md` -- where MLX numbers will land once the
  backend ships

1	Phase 1 step 001: mlpl-mlx crate skeleton + first primitive.
2	
3	Stand up the MLX runtime target as a sibling to `mlpl-rt`.
4	
5	1. New crate `crates/mlpl-mlx` with a `mlx` Cargo feature that
6	   pulls in `mlx-rs` (pin a known-good version in
7	   `Cargo.toml`). Add the crate to the workspace.
8	2. Port exactly one primitive: `matmul(a, b)`. Signature mirrors
9	   `mlpl-rt::matmul` so the two runtimes are drop-in swappable.
10	   Internally wrap MLX's `matmul` and hand back a
11	   `DenseArray` + `LabeledShape` (from `mlpl-core`) matching the
12	   CPU path's output shape and labels.
13	3. Parity test: `[8, 4] @ [4, 8]` fixture runs through both
14	   `mlpl-rt::matmul` and `mlpl-mlx::matmul` and the outputs
15	   agree bit-for-bit, or within a documented fp32 tolerance
16	   (decide during TDD and write the tolerance into the test).
17	4. Gate all MLX-backed tests behind `#[cfg(all(target_os =
18	   "macos", target_arch = "aarch64", feature = "mlx"))]` (or the
19	   closest equivalent `mlx-rs` supports). On non-Apple hosts
20	   `cargo test -p mlpl-mlx` still compiles and passes.
21	5. TDD: parity test first (red -> green -> refactor).
22	6. Quality gates + `/mw-cp`. Commit message references step 001.
23

file.content:
Phase 1 step 001: mlpl-mlx crate skeleton + first primitive.

Stand up the MLX runtime target as a sibling to `mlpl-rt`.

1. New crate `crates/mlpl-mlx` with a `mlx` Cargo feature that
   pulls in `mlx-rs` (pin a known-good version in
   `Cargo.toml`). Add the crate to the workspace.
2. Port exactly one primitive: `matmul(a, b)`. Signature mirrors
   `mlpl-rt::matmul` so the two runtimes are drop-in swappable.
   Internally wrap MLX's `matmul` and hand back a
   `DenseArray` + `LabeledShape` (from `mlpl-core`) matching the
   CPU path's output shape and labels.
3. Parity test: `[8, 4] @ [4, 8]` fixture runs through both
   `mlpl-rt::matmul` and `mlpl-mlx::matmul` and the outputs
   agree bit-for-bit, or within a documented fp32 tolerance
   (decide during TDD and write the tolerance into the test).
4. Gate all MLX-backed tests behind `#[cfg(all(target_os =
   "macos", target_arch = "aarch64", feature = "mlx"))]` (or the
   closest equivalent `mlx-rs` supports). On non-Apple hosts
   `cargo test -p mlpl-mlx` still compiles and passes.
5. TDD: parity test first (red -> green -> refactor).
6. Quality gates + `/mw-cp`. Commit message references step 001.

560	    ///   standard [broadcasting](https://swiftpackageindex.com/ml-explore/mlx-swift/main/documentation/mlx/broadcasting).
561	    ///
562	    /// # Params
563	    ///
564	    /// - other: array to multiply
565	    ///
566	    /// # Example
567	    ///
568	    /// ```rust
569	    /// use mlx_rs::Array;
570	    /// let a = Array::from_slice(&[1, 2, 3, 4], &[2, 2]);
571	    /// let b = Array::from_slice(&[-5.0, 37.5, 4., 7., 1., 0.], &[2, 3]);
572	    ///
573	    /// // produces a [2, 3] result
574	    /// let mut c = a.matmul(&b);
575	    /// ```
576	    #[default_device]
577	    pub fn matmul_device(
578	        &self,
579	        other: impl AsRef<Array>,
580	        stream: impl AsRef<Stream>,
581	    ) -> Result<Array> {
582	        Array::try_from_op(|res| unsafe {
583	            mlx_sys::mlx_matmul(
584	                res,
585	                self.as_ptr(),
586	                other.as_ref().as_ptr(),
587	                stream.as_ref().as_ptr(),
588	            )
589	        })
590	    }
591	
592	    /// Element-wise reciprocal.
593	    ///
594	    /// # Example
595	    ///
596	    /// ```rust
597	    /// use mlx_rs::Array;
598	    /// let a = Array::from_slice(&[1.0, 2.0, 4.0], &[3]);
599	    /// let mut b = a.reciprocal().unwrap();
600	    ///
601	    /// let b_data: &[f32] = b.as_slice();
602	    /// // b_data == [1.0, 0.5, 0.25]
603	    /// ```
604	    #[default_device]
605	    pub fn reciprocal_device(&self, stream: impl AsRef<Stream>) -> Result<Array> {
606	        Array::try_from_op(|res| unsafe {
607	            mlx_sys::mlx_reciprocal(res, self.as_ptr(), stream.as_ref().as_ptr())
608	        })
609	    }

content.content:
140	    /// # Parameters
141	    ///
142	    /// - `data`: A buffer which will be copied.
143	    /// - `shape`: Shape of the array.
144	    ///
145	    /// # Panic
146	    ///
147	    /// - Panics if the product of the shape is not equal to the length of the
148	    ///   data.
149	    /// - Panics if the shape is too large.
150	    pub fn from_slice<T: FromSliceElement>(data: &[T], shape: &[i32]) -> Self {
151	        // Validate data size and shape
152	        assert_eq!(data.len(), shape.iter().product::<i32>() as usize);
153	
154	        unsafe { Self::from_raw_data(data.as_ptr() as *const c_void, shape, T::DTYPE) }
155	    }
156	
157	    /// New array from a slice of f64.
158	    ///
159	    /// A separate method is provided for f64 because f64 is not supported on GPU
160	    /// and rust defaults to f64 for floating point literals
161	    pub fn from_slice_f64(data: &[f64], shape: &[i32]) -> Self {
162	        // Validate data size and shape
163	        assert_eq!(data.len(), shape.iter().product::<i32>() as usize);
164	
165	        unsafe { Self::from_raw_data(data.as_ptr() as *const c_void, shape, Dtype::Float64) }
166	    }
167	
168	    /// Create a new array from raw data buffer.
169	    ///
170	    /// This is a convenience wrapper around [`mlx_sy::mlx_array_new_data`].
171	    ///
172	    /// # Safety
173	    ///
174	    /// This is unsafe because the caller must ensure that the data buffer is valid and that the
175	    /// shape is correct.
176	    #[inline]
177	    pub unsafe fn from_raw_data(data: *const c_void, shape: &[i32], dtype: Dtype) -> Self {
178	        let dim = if shape.len() > i32::MAX as usize {
179	            panic!("Shape is too large")
180	        } else {
181	            shape.len() as i32
182	        };
183	
184	        let c_array = mlx_sys::mlx_array_new_data(data, shape.as_ptr(), dim, dtype.into());
185	        Array { c_array }
186	    }
187	
188	    /// New array from an iterator.
189	    ///
190	    /// Please note that floating point literals are treated as f32 instead of
191	    /// f64. Use [`Array::from_iter_f64`] for f64.
192	    ///
193	    /// This is a convenience method that is equivalent to
194	    ///

196	    /// let data: Vec<T> = iter.collect();

input.content:
[package]
name = "mlpl-mlx"
edition.workspace = true
license.workspace = true
version.workspace = true

[features]
default = []
# Apple Silicon only. The `cfg(all(target_os = "macos", target_arch =
# "aarch64"))` guard inside the crate keeps the feature compilable on
# non-Apple hosts (where `mlx-rs`' C++/Metal toolchain would fail to
# build), making `cargo test -p mlpl-mlx --features mlx` safe to run
# from a Linux CI host: it stubs out to a no-op surface.
mlx = ["dep:mlx-rs"]

[dependencies]
mlpl-array = { path = "../mlpl-array" }
mlpl-core = { path = "../mlpl-core" }

# Pinned by Saga 14 plan (risk/mitigation item): upstream ABI can
# shift between minor versions. Bump deliberately, not transitively.
mlx-rs = { version = "=0.25.3", optional = true }

content.text: MLX's Metal backend needs full Xcode.app (only CLT installed). Saga 14 step 001 needs the plumbing, not GPU speedup yet — switch to accelerate-only for now so the crate integrates cleanly. I'll note this caveat clearly.

input.new_string:
# Pinned by Saga 14 plan (risk/mitigation item): upstream ABI can
# shift between minor versions. Bump deliberately, not transitively.
#
# `default-features = false` drops the `metal` feature so mlx-sys
# does not require the `metal` shader compiler (ships only with a
# full Xcode.app, not the Command Line Tools). We keep
# `accelerate` on -- that's Apple's CPU/SIMD BLAS, which is all
# Saga 14 step 001 needs to prove the crate integrates. A later
# step re-enables `metal` behind its own cfg gate once the Xcode
# dependency is documented in the project bootstrap.
mlx-rs = { version = "=0.25.3", optional = true, default-features = false, features = [
  "accelerate",
] }

[package]
name = "mlpl-mlx"
edition.workspace = true
license.workspace = true
version.workspace = true

[features]
default = []
# Apple Silicon only. The `cfg(all(target_os = "macos", target_arch =
# "aarch64"))` guard inside the crate keeps the feature compilable on
# non-Apple hosts (where `mlx-rs`' C++/Metal toolchain would fail to
# build), making `cargo test -p mlpl-mlx --features mlx` safe to run
# from a Linux CI host: it stubs out to a no-op surface.
mlx = ["dep:mlx-rs"]

[dependencies]
mlpl-array = { path = "../mlpl-array" }
mlpl-core = { path = "../mlpl-core" }

# shift between minor versions. Bump deliberately, not transitively.
mlx-rs = { version = "=0.25.3", optional = true }

file.content:
FATAL_ERROR
          "Building for x86_64 on macOS is not supported."
          " If you are on an Apple silicon system, check the build"
          " documentation for possible fixes: "
          "https://ml-explore.github.io/mlx/build/html/install.html#build-from-source"
      )
    else()
      set(MLX_BUILD_METAL OFF)
      message(WARNING "Building for x86_64 arch is not officially supported.")
    endif()
  endif()

else()
  set(MLX_BUILD_METAL OFF)
  message(WARNING "MLX is prioritised for Apple silicon systems using macOS.")
endif()

# ----------------------------- Lib -----------------------------

include(FetchContent)
# Avoid warning about DOWNLOAD_EXTRACT_TIMESTAMP in CMake 3.24:
cmake_policy(SET CMP0135 NEW)

add_library(mlx)

if(MLX_BUILD_METAL)
  set(METAL_LIB "-framework Metal")
  set(FOUNDATION_LIB "-framework Foundation")
  set(QUARTZ_LIB "-framework QuartzCore")
endif()

if(MLX_BUILD_METAL AND NOT METAL_LIB)
  message(STATUS "Metal not found. Unable to build GPU")
  set(MLX_BUILD_METAL OFF)
  set(MLX_METAL_DEBUG OFF)
elseif(MLX_BUILD_METAL)
  message(STATUS "Building METAL sources")

  if(MLX_METAL_DEBUG)
    add_compile_definitions(MLX_METAL_DEBUG)
  endif()

  # Throw an error if xcrun not found
  execute_process(
    COMMAND zsh "-c" "/usr/bin/xcrun -sdk macosx --show-sdk-version"
    OUTPUT_VARIABLE MACOS_SDK_VERSION COMMAND_ERROR_IS_FATAL ANY)

  if(${MACOS_SDK_VERSION} LESS 14.0)
    message(
      FATAL_ERROR

file.content:
[package]
name = "mlpl-mlx"
edition.workspace = true
license.workspace = true
version.workspace = true

[features]
default = []
# Apple Silicon only. The `cfg(all(target_os = "macos", target_arch =
# "aarch64"))` guard inside the crate keeps the feature compilable on
# non-Apple hosts (where `mlx-rs`' C++/Metal toolchain would fail to
# build), making `cargo test -p mlpl-mlx --features mlx` safe to run
# from a Linux CI host: it stubs out to a no-op surface.
mlx = ["dep:mlx-rs"]

[dependencies]
mlpl-array = { path = "../mlpl-array" }
mlpl-core = { path = "../mlpl-core" }

# Pinned by Saga 14 plan (risk/mitigation item): upstream ABI can
# shift between minor versions. Bump deliberately, not transitively.
#
# `default-features = false` drops the `metal` feature so mlx-sys
# does not require the `metal` shader compiler (ships only with a
# full Xcode.app, not the Command Line Tools). We keep
# `accelerate` on -- that's Apple's CPU/SIMD BLAS, which is all
# Saga 14 step 001 needs to prove the crate integrates. A later
# step re-enables `metal` behind its own cfg gate once the Xcode
# dependency is documented in the project bootstrap.
mlx-rs = { version = "=0.25.3", optional = true, default-features = false, features = [
  "accelerate",
] }

content.text:
Hitting a real blocker I need input on before proceeding.

**Situation:** `mlx-rs 0.25.3` (the pinned MLX crate) transitively enables the `metal` feature on `mlx-sys`, which hard-requires the Metal shader compiler. Metal only ships with the full **Xcode.app**, not the Command Line Tools you have installed. `cmake` is now installed (I ran `brew install cmake`), but MLX's C++ build fails with `xcrun: error: unable to find utility "metal"`.

Even with `default-features = false, features = ["accelerate"]` on my end, `mlx-rs`'s own `mlx-sys` dependency line has no `default-features = false`, so Cargo's feature-union still turns on `metal`. Upstream bug in mlx-rs.

**Options:**
1. **Install Xcode.app** (~15 GB, from the App Store or `xcodes` CLI). This is the expected Saga 14 path.
2. **Patch/vendor a local `mlx-rs`** with the one-line fix (`default-features = false` on its `mlx-sys` dep) so the `accelerate`-only path works. Step 001 still lands; GPU speedup gets deferred.
3. **Pause the saga** until Xcode is available.

I was about to go with (2) for step 001 since the plan treats GPU speedup as a later-phase concern (step 008), but it's a real architectural decision — want me to proceed with the vendored patch, or should we install Xcode first?

file.content:
cmake_minimum_required(VERSION 3.25)

if(NOT MLX_VERSION)
  file(STRINGS "mlx/version.h" _mlx_h_version REGEX "^#define MLX_VERSION_.*$")
  string(REGEX MATCH "#define MLX_VERSION_MAJOR ([0-9]+)" _ "${_mlx_h_version}")
  set(_major ${CMAKE_MATCH_1})
  string(REGEX MATCH "#define MLX_VERSION_MINOR ([0-9]+)" _ "${_mlx_h_version}")
  set(_minor ${CMAKE_MATCH_1})
  string(REGEX MATCH "#define MLX_VERSION_PATCH ([0-9]+)" _ "${_mlx_h_version}")
  set(_patch ${CMAKE_MATCH_1})
  set(MLX_PROJECT_VERSION "${_major}.${_minor}.${_patch}")
  set(MLX_VERSION ${MLX_PROJECT_VERSION})
else()
  string(REGEX REPLACE "^([0-9]+\.[0-9]+\.[0-9]+).*" "\\1" MLX_PROJECT_VERSION
                       ${MLX_VERSION})
endif()

project(
  mlx
  LANGUAGES C CXX
  VERSION ${MLX_PROJECT_VERSION})

# ----------------------------- Setup -----------------------------
set(CMAKE_MODULE_PATH "${PROJECT_SOURCE_DIR}/cmake")
set(CMAKE_CXX_STANDARD 17)
set(CMAKE_CXX_STANDARD_REQUIRED ON)
set(CMAKE_POSITION_INDEPENDENT_CODE ON)
set(CMAKE_INSTALL_MESSAGE NEVER)

# ----------------------------- Configuration -----------------------------
option(MLX_BUILD_TESTS "Build tests for mlx" ON)
option(MLX_BUILD_EXAMPLES "Build examples for mlx" ON)
option(MLX_BUILD_BENCHMARKS "Build benchmarks for mlx" OFF)
option(MLX_BUILD_PYTHON_BINDINGS "Build python bindings for mlx" OFF)
option(MLX_BUILD_METAL "Build metal backend" ON)
option(MLX_BUILD_CPU "Build cpu backend" ON)
option(MLX_METAL_DEBUG "Enhance metal debug workflow" OFF)
option(MLX_ENABLE_X64_MAC "Enable building for x64 macOS" OFF)
option(MLX_BUILD_GGUF "Include support for GGUF format" ON)
option(MLX_BUILD_SAFETENSORS "Include support for safetensors format" ON)
option(MLX_BUILD_BLAS_FROM_SOURCE "Build OpenBLAS from source code" OFF)
option(MLX_METAL_JIT "Use JIT compilation for Metal kernels" OFF)
option(BUILD_SHARED_LIBS "Build mlx as a shared library" OFF)

# --------------------- Processor tests -------------------------
message(
  STATUS
    "Building MLX for ${CMAKE_SYSTEM_PROCESSOR} processor on ${CMAKE_SYSTEM_NAME}"
)

if(${CMAKE_SYSTEM_NAME} MATCHES "Darwin")
  if(${CMAKE_SYSTEM_PROCESSOR} MATCHES "x86_64")
    if(NOT MLX_ENABLE_X64_MAC)
      message(
        FATAL_ERROR
          "Building for x86_64 on macOS is not supported."
          " If you are on an Apple silicon system, check the build"
          " documentation for possible fixes: "
          "https://ml-explore.github.io/mlx/build/html/install.html#build-from-source"
      )
    else()
      set(MLX_BUILD_METAL OFF)
      message(WARNING "Building for x86_64 arch is not officially supported.")
    endif()
  endif()

else()
  set(MLX_BUILD_METAL OFF)
  message(WARNING "MLX is prioritised for Apple silicon systems using macOS.")
endif()

# ----------------------------- Lib -----------------------------

include(FetchContent)
# Avoid warning about DOWNLOAD_EXTRACT_TIMESTAMP in CMake 3.24:
cmake_policy(SET CMP0135 NEW)

add_library(mlx)

if(MLX_BUILD_METAL)
  set(METAL_LIB "-framework Metal")
  set(FOUNDATION_LIB "-framework Foundation")
  set(QUARTZ_LIB "-framework QuartzCore")
endif()

if(MLX_BUILD_METAL AND NOT METAL_LIB)
  message(STATUS "Metal not found. Unable to build GPU")
  set(MLX_BUILD_METAL OFF)
  set(MLX_METAL_DEBUG OFF)
elseif(MLX_BUILD_METAL)
  message(STATUS "Building METAL sources")

  if(MLX_METAL_DEBUG)
    add_compile_definitions(MLX_METAL_DEBUG)
  endif()

  # Throw an error if xcrun not found
  execute_process(
    COMMAND zsh "-c" "/usr/bin/xcrun -sdk macosx --show-sdk-version"
    OUTPUT_VARIABLE MACOS_SDK_VERSION COMMAND_ERROR_IS_FATAL ANY)

  if(${MACOS_SDK_VERSION} LESS 14.0)
    message(
      FATAL_ERROR
        "MLX requires macOS SDK >= 14.0 to be built with MLX_BUILD_METAL=ON")
  endif()
  message(STATUS "Building with macOS SDK version ${MACOS_SDK_VERSION}")

  set(METAL_CPP_URL
      https://developer.apple.com/metal/cpp/files/metal-cpp_macOS15_iOS18.zip)

  if(NOT CMAKE_OSX_DEPLOYMENT_TARGET STREQUAL "")
    set(XCRUN_FLAGS "-mmacosx-version-min=${CMAKE_OSX_DEPLOYMENT_TARGET}")
  endif()
  execute_process(
    COMMAND
      zsh "-c"
      "echo \"__METAL_VERSION__\" | xcrun -sdk macosx metal ${XCRUN_FLAGS} -E -x metal -P - | tail -1 | tr -d '\n'"
    OUTPUT_VARIABLE MLX_METAL_VERSION COMMAND_ERROR_IS_FATAL ANY)
  FetchContent_Declare(metal_cpp URL ${METAL_CPP_URL})

content.text:
Diagnosis complete — no guessing needed.

**What's installed:**
- Command Line Tools 15.5 (has clang/make/etc., **no** `metal`, `metallib`, or `metal-ar`)
- `cmake 4.3.1` (via Homebrew, just added)
- Python MLX wheels (`mlx-metal 0.30.6`, `mlx 0.30.6`) cached in `~/.cache/uv/archive-v0/` — these ship precompiled `libmlx.dylib` + `mlx.metallib`
- No Xcode.app; no Metal Developer Tools

**What your prior MLX work used:** All Python (`~/github/softwarewrighter/mlx-play/unsloth-demo/requirements.txt` and `mHC-poc/mlx/requirements.txt`). Python wheels ship pre-built binaries, so Metal compiler was never needed locally.

**What mlx-rs 0.25.3 actually needs:** It builds MLX from source via CMake. MLX's main `CMakeLists.txt` cleanly skips all Metal code when `MLX_BUILD_METAL=OFF` (lines 80–287 all gated). So **if we can force `MLX_BUILD_METAL=OFF`, zero Xcode bits are needed** — Accelerate-only build works against the CLT toolchain you already have.

**Why my `default-features = false` didn't work:** mlx-rs's own `Cargo.toml` at line 27 (`[dependencies.mlx-sys] version = "=0.2.0"`) omits `default-features = false`. Cargo's feature-union then re-enables `mlx-sys/default` (= accelerate + metal) regardless of what I set. It's an upstream packaging bug in `mlx-rs`.

**Proposal** (low-disk, no installs): vendor `mlx-rs 0.25.3` locally at `vendor/mlx-rs/` (~1.2MB of Rust source) with a one-line Cargo.toml fix, then `[patch.crates-io] mlx-rs = { path = "vendor/mlx-rs" }` in the workspace. This builds MLX with Accelerate only — valid MLX, real `mlx_rs::Array::matmul`, passes the step 001 parity test. GPU/Metal dispatch gets its own step later when we're ready to sort out the Metal toolchain question separately.

Want me to proceed with the vendored-patch path?

input.content:
# Vendored mlx-rs 0.25.3

This directory holds an as-published copy of the `mlx-rs` v0.25.3
crate (MIT OR Apache-2.0, same as this repo) with **one line**
changed from upstream.

## Why vendored

`mlx-rs` 0.25.3 declares its `mlx-sys` dependency as:

```toml
[dependencies.mlx-sys]
version = "=0.2.0"
```

without `default-features = false`. Because `mlx-sys`'s default
feature set is `["accelerate", "metal"]`, Cargo's feature-union
rule keeps the `metal` feature on for `mlx-sys` no matter what
downstream crates (including `mlpl-mlx`) request. The `metal`
feature forces the MLX C++ CMake build to compile Metal shaders,
which in turn requires the `metal` / `metallib` shader compilers
that ship **only** with the full `Xcode.app` -- not the Command
Line Tools most Rust developers have.

For the MLPL Saga 14 step 001 scope (plumbing + parity test on
`matmul`) the Accelerate CPU/SIMD backend is enough: we're proving
that MLPL can swap `mlpl-rt` for `mlpl-mlx` at the primitive
boundary, not measuring GPU speedup (that's step 008).

## What changed

Exactly one edit to `Cargo.toml`:

```diff
 [dependencies.mlx-sys]
 version = "=0.2.0"
+default-features = false
```

Source code, build scripts, and Cargo.lock are identical to the
published crate. `Cargo.toml.orig` is the upstream pre-normalization
`Cargo.toml` and is kept verbatim for reference.

## How the workspace consumes it

Top-level `Cargo.toml` has:

```toml
[patch.crates-io]
mlx-rs = { path = "vendor/mlx-rs" }
```

which replaces the crates.io copy of `mlx-rs` with this vendored
one during dependency resolution. Everything that depends on
`mlx-rs` (currently just `crates/mlpl-mlx`) picks up the fixed
`mlx-sys` dependency transparently.

## Upstream fix

This is an upstream packaging bug. When `mlx-rs` publishes a
release with the one-line fix (or the `mlx-sys` author removes
`metal` from `mlx-sys`'s default features), this directory can be
deleted and the `[patch.crates-io]` entry dropped.

Tracking: file an issue at <https://github.com/oxideai/mlx-rs>
if one is not already open.

Markdown Checker - Validate and Auto-Fix Markdown Files

PURPOSE:
  Ensures markdown files contain only UTF-8 encoded, ASCII-subset characters
  without unprintable control characters or non-standard tree visualization symbols.
  Helps maintain portable, accessible documentation that works across all editors,
  terminals, CI/CD pipelines, and accessibility tools.

CAPABILITIES:
  • UTF-8 Validation: Ensures files are valid UTF-8 encoded text
  • ASCII Subset: Detects any character outside ASCII range (0-127)
  • Unprintable Detection: Finds control characters and other unprintable bytes
  • Tree Symbol Detection: Identifies box-drawing characters (U+2500-U+257F)
  • Auto-Fix: Automatically replaces tree symbols with ASCII equivalents
  • Dry-Run Mode: Preview fixes before applying them
  • Glob Patterns: Process multiple files matching a pattern
  • Detailed Reporting: Shows exact line/column numbers for violations

VALIDATION RULES:
  ✓ Valid: ASCII characters (32-126) plus whitespace (space, tab, LF, CR)
  ✗ Invalid: Unicode characters, emojis, accents, box-drawing symbols

  Auto-fixable: Tree symbols, checkmarks, arrows, accented letters, smart quotes, etc.
    Examples: tree chars to +|-/-, checkmarks to [x], arrows to ->/etc, accents to plain
  Not auto-fixable: Emojis (except common ones), unprintable control characters

USAGE PATTERNS:
  # Validate single file
  markdown-checker                          # Checks ./README.md (or README.org if README.md not found)
  markdown-checker -f CONTRIBUTING.md       # Checks ./CONTRIBUTING.md
  markdown-checker -p docs -f overview.md   # Checks docs/overview.md

  # Validate multiple files with glob patterns
  markdown-checker -f "*.md"              # All .md files in current dir
  markdown-checker -p docs -f "**/*.md"   # All .md files in docs/ recursively

  # Auto-fix tree symbols
  markdown-checker --fix                    # Fix violations in README.md
  markdown-checker -f "*.md" --fix        # Fix all .md files

5	---
6	
7	# /mw-cp — Mike Wright's Checkpoint Process
8	
9	You are executing a disciplined checkpoint commit. Follow every phase in order.
10	Do NOT skip any phase, even if it seems clean. Report status after each phase.
11	
12	---
13	
14	## Phase 1 — Pre-Commit Checks
15	
16	Run all of the following that apply to this project. Stop and report failures before proceeding.
17	
18	1. **Format**: `cargo fmt --check` (or `rustfmt --check` on specific files)
19	2. **Lint**: `cargo clippy -- -D warnings`
20	3. **Build**: `cargo build` (debug is fine; surface any compile errors)
21	4. **Tests**: `cargo test` — all tests must pass
22	5. **Unused deps** (if `cargo-machete` present): `cargo machete`
23	6. **Project-local checks**: run `just check` or `just pre-commit` if a justfile exists
24	
25	If any check fails, STOP. Report what failed and ask the user how to proceed.
26	
27	---
28	
29	## Phase 2 — Documentation Updates
30	
31	Review recent changes and update the following as appropriate:
32	
33	1. **CHANGELOG.md / CHANGES.md** — add an entry under `[Unreleased]` or today's date
34	2. **STATUS.md** — update current state, what changed, what's in progress
35	3. **LEARNINGS.md** — capture anything notable: decisions made, gotchas, tradeoffs
36	4. **Inline docs** — ensure any new public API items have doc comments (`///`)
37	5. **README.md** — update if any user-facing behavior, config, or setup changed
38	
39	If none of these files exist, note that and skip gracefully.
40	
41	---

44	
45	Run `git diff --staged` (or `git diff HEAD` if nothing staged yet).
46	
47	Summarize what is changing:
48	- Files modified, added, deleted
49	- High-level functional description of the change
50	
51	Then stage all changes: `git add -A`

56

58	```
59	<type>(<scope>): <short imperative summary>
60	
61	<body — explain WHAT changed and WHY, not HOW>
62	<mention any tradeoffs, alternatives considered, or follow-ups needed>
63

71	
72	Execute: `git commit -m "<message>"`

77	
78	1. Check current branch: `git branch --show-current`
79	2. Check remote: `git remote -v`
80	3. Push: `git push origin <branch>`
81	
82	If the push is rejected (non-fast-forward), STOP and report — do NOT force push without explicit user confirmation.
83

87

92	- 🚀 Pushed to remote/branch

file.content:
---
allowed-tools: Bash(git *), Bash(cargo *), Bash(clippy *), Bash(rustfmt *), Bash(just *)
description: Mike Wright's Checkpoint Process — pre-commit checks, doc updates, detailed commit, and push
argument-hint: [optional commit message context]
---

# /mw-cp — Mike Wright's Checkpoint Process

You are executing a disciplined checkpoint commit. Follow every phase in order.
Do NOT skip any phase, even if it seems clean. Report status after each phase.

---

## Phase 1 — Pre-Commit Checks

Run all of the following that apply to this project. Stop and report failures before proceeding.

1. **Format**: `cargo fmt --check` (or `rustfmt --check` on specific files)
2. **Lint**: `cargo clippy -- -D warnings`
3. **Build**: `cargo build` (debug is fine; surface any compile errors)
4. **Tests**: `cargo test` — all tests must pass
5. **Unused deps** (if `cargo-machete` present): `cargo machete`
6. **Project-local checks**: run `just check` or `just pre-commit` if a justfile exists

If any check fails, STOP. Report what failed and ask the user how to proceed.

---

## Phase 2 — Documentation Updates

Review recent changes and update the following as appropriate:

1. **CHANGELOG.md / CHANGES.md** — add an entry under `[Unreleased]` or today's date
2. **STATUS.md** — update current state, what changed, what's in progress
3. **LEARNINGS.md** — capture anything notable: decisions made, gotchas, tradeoffs
4. **Inline docs** — ensure any new public API items have doc comments (`///`)
5. **README.md** — update if any user-facing behavior, config, or setup changed

If none of these files exist, note that and skip gracefully.

---

## Phase 3 — Staged Diff Review

Run `git diff --staged` (or `git diff HEAD` if nothing staged yet).

Summarize what is changing:
- Files modified, added, deleted
- High-level functional description of the change

Then stage all changes: `git add -A`

---

## Phase 4 — Commit

Compose a detailed commit message following Conventional Commits style:
```
<type>(<scope>): <short imperative summary>

<body — explain WHAT changed and WHY, not HOW>
<mention any tradeoffs, alternatives considered, or follow-ups needed>

Refs: <issue/ticket numbers if applicable>
Co-authored-by: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>
```

Types: `feat`, `fix`, `refactor`, `docs`, `test`, `chore`, `perf`, `build`

If $ARGUMENTS was provided, incorporate it as context for the message.

Execute: `git commit -m "<message>"`

---

## Phase 5 — Push

1. Check current branch: `git branch --show-current`
2. Check remote: `git remote -v`
3. Push: `git push origin <branch>`

If the push is rejected (non-fast-forward), STOP and report — do NOT force push without explicit user confirmation.

---

## Phase 6 — Checkpoint Summary

Report a brief summary:
- ✅ Checks passed
- 📝 Docs updated (list files)
- 🔖 Commit hash and message
- 🚀 Pushed to remote/branch

Flag any items that were skipped or need follow-up.
```

---

## Usage
```

content.content:
default:
    @just --list

check:
    cargo check

test:
    cargo test

fmt:
    cargo fmt --all

clippy:
    cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings
---
name: Deploy to GitHub Pages

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  workflow_dispatch:

permissions:
  contents: read
  pages: write
  id-token: write

concurrency:
  group: pages
  cancel-in-progress: true

jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment:
      name: github-pages
      url: ${{ steps.deployment.outputs.page_url }}
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/upload-pages-artifact@v3
        with:
          path: ./pages
      - id: deployment
        uses: actions/deploy-pages@v4

content.content:
CLI tool for validating Software Wrighter LLC project conformance

Usage: sw-checklist [OPTIONS] [PATH]

Arguments:
  [PATH]
          Project path to check (defaults to current directory)
          
          [default: .]

Options:
  -v, --verbose
          Show per-check details (default: summary only)

  -h, --help
          Print help (see a summary with '-h')

  -V, --version
          Print version

AI CODING AGENT INSTRUCTIONS:

This tool validates that projects conform to Software Wrighter LLC standards.

USAGE FOR AI AGENTS:
  1. Run this tool on any project to get a checklist of requirements
  2. Address each issue reported by the tool
  3. Re-run to verify all checks pass

CHECKS PERFORMED:
  - Rust edition must be 2024
  - Clippy allows: fails if #[allow(clippy::...)] directives found
  - Functions: warns if >25 LOC, fails if >50 LOC
  - Modules: warns if >4 functions, fails if >7 functions
  - Crates: warns if >4 modules, fails if >7 modules
  - CLI binaries: help/version output validation
  - Web UI: favicon, index.html, footer requirements

IMPORTANT: Do not disable clippy checks with #[allow(clippy::...)] attributes.
Fix the underlying issues instead. Clippy warnings exist to improve code quality.

## Planned

| # | Saga | Target | Status | Depends on |
|---|------|--------|--------|------------|
| 14 | MLX backend (Apple Silicon, lazy graph, fusion) | v0.11 | [ ] | 13 |
| 15 | LoRA / QLoRA / quantization | v0.12 | [ ] | 13 |
| 16 | Embedding visualization and RAG | v0.13 | [ ] | 13 |
| 17 | CUDA backend and distributed execution | v0.14 | [ ] | 14 |
| 18 | Distillation, ICL/ICRL, engram memory, orchestration | v0.15 | [ ] | 15 |
| 19 | LLM-as-tool REST integration | v0.16 | [ ] | 12 |

## Next saga to start

**Saga 13 -- Tiny language model end-to-end (CPU).** Three
surface-only sagas shipped in rapid succession on top of Saga 11
(Model DSL): Saga 11.5 added labeled shapes and structured
errors (v0.7.5); the Compile-to-Rust saga added the `mlpl!` proc
macro + `mlpl build` native-binary CLI (v0.8.0, 9x measured
speedup); Saga 12 added filesystem IO (`load`, `--data-dir`
sandbox), dataset prep (`shuffle`/`batch`/`split`/`for`), a
byte-level BPE tokenizer (`tokenize_bytes`, `train_bpe`,
`apply_tokenizer`, `decode`), and experiment tracking
(`experiment "name" { ... }`, `:experiments`, `compare(a, b)`)
in v0.9.0 (plus a byproduct UTF-8 lexer fix that lets string
literals carry arbitrary Unicode). With both shape-checked
tensors and a full text-to-tokens pipeline in place, Saga 13
trains a tiny transformer-block LM end-to-end on a small
corpus: tokenize -> batch -> Model DSL with `attention` and
`residual` -> `adam` in a `train { }` loop -> sampling loop.

input.command:
git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
feat(mlpl-mlx): Saga 14 step 001 -- MLX runtime target + matmul

Stand up the `mlpl-mlx` runtime crate as a sibling to `mlpl-rt`
and port the first primitive (`matmul`) to MLX via `mlx-rs`.
Programs that call into `mlpl_rt::DenseArray::matmul` can now
swap to `mlpl_mlx::matmul` and get the same result (within f32
tolerance) on Apple Silicon.

What ships:
- `crates/mlpl-mlx/` -- new workspace member with an `mlx` Cargo
  feature gate plus a triple `cfg(all(target_os = "macos",
  target_arch = "aarch64", feature = "mlx"))` on the actual
  primitive. On any other configuration the crate still compiles
  (no-op surface), satisfying the Saga 14 invariant that
  cross-platform CI stays green.
- `mlpl_mlx::matmul(a, b)` -- wraps `mlx_rs::Array::matmul` and
  converts DenseArray <-> MlxArray in f32. Shape validation,
  rank checks, and label propagation mirror
  `DenseArray::matmul` byte-for-byte, so error cases match the
  CPU path as well as success cases.
- Parity test harness (`tests/parity_tests.rs`) covering the
  spec fixture `[8, 4] @ [4, 8]`, matrix-vector matmul,
  Saga 11.5 label propagation, contraction-axis label mismatch,
  and dim mismatch. Numerical tolerance is 1e-5 absolute (f32
  round-trip ~ O(k) * eps_f32 for k = 4).

Vendored patch:
- `vendor/mlx-rs/` holds an as-published copy of `mlx-rs` 0.25.3
  with one Cargo.toml line changed: `[dependencies.mlx-sys]` now
  sets `default-features = false`. Upstream forgets this, so
  Cargo's feature-union keeps `mlx-sys/metal` on regardless of
  what downstream requests, which in turn demands the `metal`
  shader compiler that ships only with the full `Xcode.app`.
  The vendored fix lets the Accelerate backend build against
  the Command Line Tools toolchain alone. `[patch.crates-io]`
  in the workspace root points at the local copy. See
  `vendor/mlx-rs/VENDORING.md` for the full rationale and the
  upstream-fix path.

Scope explicitly deferred (per Saga 14 plan):
- GPU / Metal dispatch (step 008's speedup gate). Accelerate-
  only is fine for step 001's parity goal.
- The rest of the primitive surface -- add / sub / mul /
  reduce_add / softmax / ... -- lands in steps 002-003.
- `device("mlx") { }` scoped form and Model DSL dispatch -- 004-005.
- Autograd, optimizers, Tiny LM, benches, tutorial, release --
  006-010.

Quality gates:
- `cargo test` -- 122 test binaries green, including 5 new parity
  tests in `mlpl-mlx` under `--features mlx`.
- `cargo clippy --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings` --
  zero warnings.
- `cargo fmt --all -- --check` -- clean.
- markdown-checker -- clean on every project-owned `*.md`
  (vendor/ is upstream content and not in scope for our lint).
- `sw-checklist` -- `mlpl-mlx` passes every check with a single
  WARN on `matmul`'s 27-line function body (two match arms, no
  reasonable split). Pre-existing project failures unchanged;
  111 additional failures are from the vendored upstream crate
  and are not our code to fix.

Refs: Saga 14 plan step 001, `docs/milestone-mlx-backend.md`

Co-authored-by: Claude Opus 4.7 <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)" 2>&1 | tail -10
